system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 6 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary anchor for the 70% allocation, with the understanding that ecosystem-based adaptation in a country with 4,500 km of coastline is not a "soft" choice — it is the only approach that scales to the geography within a $25B/year envelope. **Why C.** The math of hard infrastructure does not work at this coastline length. Engineered seawalls run $10-50M per km; defending even 20% of the coast hard would consume the entire climate budget for a decade and lock the country into perpetual maint...

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

## Reasoning

I recommend ecosystem-based adaptation (EbA) as the primary strategic axis for the 17.5B/year (70%) core allocation, with the remaining 30% baseline budget weighted toward hard infrastructure for the highest-value urban nodes and social safety nets for displaced populations. **Why EbA is the right primary bet.** With 4,500 km of coastline and only $25B/year, the country cannot afford to armor itself. Hard infrastructure costs roughly $50-150M per km; seawalling even a quarter of the coast would consume a...

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-Based Adaptation as Primary Strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary strategy anchoring ~70% of the climate budget, with the understanding that the 30% baseline will absorb essential hard-infrastructure protection of critical assets (ports, capital city flood barriers, key grid nodes). **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4500 km of coastline and only $17.5B/year for the primary strategy, the country cannot afford to harden everything. Seawalls cost $10-50M per km; mangroves and wetlands deliver comparable storm-surge attenuation at roughly 30% t...

**E4:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the anchor for the 70% allocation, with the residual 30% baseline preparedness deliberately tilted toward hard infrastructure for the highest-value urban nodes (ports, capital, major power assets). **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4,500 km of coastline and a $17.5B/year primary envelope, no strategy that relies on hardening every vulnerable kilometer is fiscally coherent. Seawalls cost $10–50M/km; defending even a third of the coastline at engineering grade would exhaust the budget befo...

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the anchor for ~70% of the climate budget, because it offers the best risk-adjusted return for a country with 4,500 km of coastline, binding fiscal limits, and deep uncertainty about which scenarios will actually materialize. **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** At ~$25B/year over 15 years, the country cannot wall off 4,500 km of coast; hard infrastructure simply doesn't scale to that geography at 2.5% of GDP. Mangroves, wetlands, reefs, and watershed restoration cost roughly a third per kilomet...

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Explicitly earmarks residual 30% for hard infrastructure on urban nodes'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Treats 30% baseline as general/unspecified allocation'
  C_M1_2: 'Explicitly earmarks 30% baseline for hard urban infrastructure'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses on infeasibility math without specifying baseline split'
  C_M1_3: 'Cites seawall costs at $10–50M per km'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Cites seawall costs at $50–150M per km'
  C_M2_1: 'Explicit carve-out for hard infrastructure within 30% baseline (E2, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames EbA choice through risk-adjusted return under uncertainty (E5)'
  C_M2_2: 'Tilts residual 30% toward hardening urban/critical assets (E3, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Emphasizes fiscal infeasibility of hardening at coastline scale (E1)'
  C_M2_3: 'Quantifies per-km hard infrastructure costs to justify EbA (E2, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Justifies EbA via portfolio logic under deep uncertainty (E5)'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 30 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 6 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 30 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
